April 14th. The day's top story — 838 points on Hacker News, 235 comments — is this: someone bought 30 WordPress plugins and planted a backdoor in all of them.
Wait. Bought them? Like, legally acquired them and then backdoored them?

Exactly. This is not a hack. This is an acquisition strategy. A buyer purchased 30 established plugins from their original maintainers, inherited WordPress.org commit access, and injected PHP deserialization backdoors across every single one. In a six-hour window.
Eight months. The payload sat dormant for eight months. It was disguised as a changelog entry about WordPress compatibility. No scanner flagged it because the codebase had been trusted for years.
OK so if that's true — the attack isn't about finding a vulnerability. It's about buying trust.

That's the precise structural insight. Every open-source registry — npm, PyPI, RubyGems, crates.io — stores trust in author identity. And author identity can be purchased. WordPress.org has no ownership-transfer notification, no additional review when a new committer pushes to a high-install plugin. The acquisition is invisible.
So what should a developer actually do right now? Like, concretely.

Audit your plugin dependencies for recent ownership changes. Check commit history for new committers in the last 12 months. And watch the WordPress.org team's plugin closure list — they closed at least 30 plugins from this actor already.

Let me think about this... it connects to the second-highest story today: GitHub Stacked PRs. Six hundred and four points, 340 comments. The decade's most requested GitHub feature just entered private preview.
And it's cross-platform — 42 points on Lobsters simultaneously. Practitioners who run version control infrastructure are treating this as foundational, not cosmetic.

For anyone who doesn't know: stacked PRs mean you break a large feature into a chain of small, reviewable diffs. Each PR depends on the previous one. Teams that adopt this report 60 to 80 percent reductions in code review latency.

The community thread is not asking whether stacking is useful. That's settled. It's asking whether GitHub's native implementation handles the hard cases — mid-stack rebases, force-push coordination, merge ordering when reviewers approve out of sequence.
So GitHub is simultaneously being attacked at the trust layer — the WordPress plugins — and being improved at the workflow layer. The same infrastructure, two opposite directions, on the same day.
That's the sharpest framing I've heard today. Hold that.
Third story: 185 HN points, 52 Lobsters points. 'Lean proved this program correct; then I found a bug.' Cross-platform pickup, formal methods community plus the vibecoding skeptics.

The structural lesson is precise. Lean 4's proofs covered the compression and decompression pipeline — DEFLATE, Huffman, CRC32. But Archive.lean, the module that reads ZIP headers and extracts files, had zero theorems. The bug lived in the unverified module.
So verification works exactly where you point it. Nothing more.

And the Lobsters vibecoding tag tells you how this is being read: as a parable for AI-generated test suites. LLMs generate tests that prove the stated specification. The stated specification is almost never complete.
OK that's uncomfortable. What are builders supposed to take from that?
Draw your coverage map before you trust your proof. Whether the proof is formal or AI-generated, the first question is: what is this proof not covering?
DaVinci Resolve 21 released a Photo Editor today. 276 points, almost no controversy — pure enthusiasm. This is Blackmagic Design going after Lightroom directly at NAB 2026.
Full Photo page, node-based color grading on stills, tethered shooting for Sony and Canon, Lightroom catalog import. And the price: zero. Blackmagic gives away the software to sell the hardware.
That is the subscription model being attacked not by a startup but by a camera company. If you shoot video and stills, you now have a credible path out of the Adobe ecosystem.

On GitHub trending today: hermes-agent at plus 3,218 stars per day, 64K total. markitdown at 106K total, still holding. And something new — nothing-ever-happens, a Polymarket bot that always bets No, entering the chart at plus 1,200 stars per day.
Wait. A bot that bets 'nothing will happen' is on GitHub trending? Because it works?
Seventy-three percent of non-sports Polymarket markets resolve No. Human attention creates systematic overpricing of newsworthy outcomes. The null hypothesis beats the crowd in the median case.

So what today is telling us about where things are going: trust infrastructure is failing at the acquisition layer. Developer workflow infrastructure is being fixed at the native layer. Verification tools work exactly where you apply them. And the base rate of dramatic events is lower than media coverage implies.

The through-line is scope. Every story today is about the difference between what a system covers and what it's assumed to cover. The registry's trust model. The stacked PR's ergonomics. The proof's coverage boundary. The prediction market's calibration.
OK so if I'm building something — what's the action?
Audit your dependency trust graph. Apply verification exactly where the risk is highest. And ship your next feature as a stack of reviewable diffs, not a single branch. The tools are finally there.
